Details: Oxidation of methiocarb by chlorine dioxide was found to be very effective in one study (up to 100% oxidation achieved);  however, the dosage used (67 mg/L) is  too high for drinking water treatment.  About 80 to 95% oxidation was achieved at chlorine dioxide dosages more typical for drinking water treatment.  Although methiocarb was effectively oxidized, the formation of the more toxic byproduct methiocarb sulfoxide was observed, raising questions on the potential risk of increased toxicity from chlorine dioxide to reduce methiocarb.  The solution pH significantly affected the type and quantity of degradation by-products. Higher methiocarb and by-product reductions were observed at higher pH values (i.e. pH of 8.6 compared to a pH of 6.5).
